original_abstract: The selective, direct functionalization of methane into high-value chemicals remains a paramount challenge in modern chemistry. Currently, acetic acid is produced industrially via the methanol carbonylation process, which requires expensive homogeneous catalysts and poses significant environmental challenges. In this work, we present a novel photocatalytic pathway for the room-temperature synthesis of acetic acid from CH₄ and CO, utilizing water as the sole external oxygen source. Our optimized photocatalyst features a TiO₂ support decorated with ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate (NPW) clusters and anchored with isolated platinum single atoms (Pt₁). This system achieves the stable production of a 5.7 mmol·L⁻¹ acetic acid solution over 60 hours, reaching a liquid-phase selectivity of over 90% and a carbon-basis selectivity of 66%, with a turnover of 99 moles of acetic acid per mole of platinum. Through a combination of isotopic labeling and in situ spectroscopy, we demonstrate that the reaction proceeds via the photocatalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane at the Pt₁ sites, with methane activation being significantly enhanced by water-derived hydroxyl radicals.
humanized_abstract: The direct selective functionalization of methane into valuable chemicals is a longstanding challenge in the field of catalysis. The production of acetic acid currently relies on the well-known and highlyenergycostly methanol carbonylation route using expensive homogeneous transition metal catalysts and is a highly environmentally conflicting process. Here, we develop and demonstrate a photocatalytic strategy for the selective conversion of CH 4 into acetic acid at ambient temperature using CO and water as feedstocks. At room temperature and under ambient pressure, using TiO 2 as a catalyst support and modifying it with ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate (NPW) clusters and isolated single platinum atoms, we achieve the stable production of up to 5.7 mmol·L −1 acetic acid solution over 60 hours (based on liquid phase selectivity >90% and based on carbon atoms, 66% and an overall platinum catalyst turnover of 99 acetic acid moles catalyst −1 ). The influence of individual components of the catalyst and of water on the latter crucial for catalyst stability on the methane conversion and overall product selectivity is elucidated by using 13 CH 4 and in situ spectroscopy, thus showing that the overall process of the photocatalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane at Pt sites for acetic acid production is greatly enhanced by the catalyst-active sites synergy with water-derived hydroxyl radical species, which strongly enhance the methane activation process at the Pt 1 sites.
